title: The Deconstruction of Christianity, Part 2: Rerun and Fallout
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: 1-26-25 - The Deconstruction of Christianity - Part 2 - Rerun and Fallout - Elder Nick Anderson.txt

outline.title: Review: Defining Deconstruction
subpoints: Faith deconstruction defined as a postmodern process of rethinking faith without regarding Scripture as a standard
Examining faith is good and necessary, but must be done in light of Scripture
The Reformed motto: Ecclesia reformata, semper reformanda — always reforming according to the Word of God
Derrida's origin of the term: dismantling loyalty to ideas to find truth in the opposite

outline.title: The Rerun: Deconstruction Is Nothing New
subpoints: Ecclesiastes 1:9 — 'there is nothing new under the sun'
Deconstruction finds its origin in the Fall, specifically Genesis 3
Satan's strategy: question God's word, deny God's word, deconstruct who God is
Satan questions: 'Has God indeed said...?' — an ancient pattern repeated in every age

outline.title: Satan's Three-Step Deconstruction
subpoints: Step 1: Question God's word — 'Has God indeed said...?'
Step 2: Deny God's word — 'You will not surely die'
Step 3: Deconstruct God himself — God is withholding good, God is untruthful, God is insufficient
Romans 1:25 — people 'exchanged the truth of God for the lie' of autonomy

outline.title: Old Testament and New Testament Examples
subpoints: Jeremiah 19: Israel let cultural practices of pagan nations define their worship, even offering children to Baal
We tend to let culture define Christianity rather than the Bible
Paul warns Timothy about those who have wandered away, made shipwreck of their faith, departed from the faith
The church today is losing the knowledge of God — Christians quote the Office but can't find their way through the Bible

outline.title: The Fallout: Real Damage from Deconstruction
subpoints: Deconstruction leads to death of beliefs, community, confidence, and relationships
The stories promoted are of happiness, but real people experience pain and discouragement
Deconstruction affects not just the individual but family members, friends, and the church
Deconstruction coaches are instruments of Satan, not friends — Jesus warns of millstone judgment for those who cause little ones to stumble
Abraham Piper (John Piper's son) now evangelizes for deconstruction

summary:
Pastor Tito Lyro teaches the second lesson in the five-part Deconstruction of Christianity series. The lesson is organized around two themes: the 'rerun' (deconstruction is nothing new) and the 'fallout' (its real and devastating consequences). He opens with Ecclesiastes 1:9-10 — 'there is nothing new under the sun' — to frame the argument that deconstruction, however new the terminology, is an ancient phenomenon rooted in the Fall itself.

Pastor Lyro reviews the definition from the previous lesson and walks through Genesis 3 in detail, showing that Satan's approach to Eve is the template for all deconstruction: first, question God's word ('Has God indeed said...?'), then deny God's word ('You will not surely die'), and finally, deconstruct God himself by claiming he is withholding good, is untruthful, and is insufficient. Modern deconstruction, he argues, repeats these same moves in contemporary cultural idiom. He also draws on Jeremiah 19, where Israel adopted the religious practices of surrounding pagan nations and even sacrificed children in the Temple, as a historical example of allowing culture to override Scripture.

The second half of the lesson addresses the fallout from deconstruction. Despite the celebratory narratives promoted on social media, deconstruction causes real harm: to the person deconstructing (loss of faith, community, and direction), to their families, and to the broader church. Pastor Lyro draws a parallel with the transgender movement — both project stories of liberation and happiness, while actual participants often experience profound pain. He specifically identifies deconstruction coaches as dangerous, citing Jesus's warning about millstones for those who cause little ones to stumble, and noting that Abraham Piper (son of John Piper) now actively evangelizes for deconstruction. The church's responsibility is not merely to accompany people on their 'journey' but to tell them where they need to end up.
transcript:
## Opening and Introduction

We are considering the subject of the deconstruction of Christianity in our Sunday school class — this is our second lesson. There will be five of them. Nick and I will be teaching these five lessons. And I wanted to start our lesson today by reading a passage from Ecclesiastes. Ecclesiastes chapter 1 verses 9 and 10. There Solomon by the inspiration of the Holy Spirit says that which has been is what will be, that which is done is what will be done and there's nothing new under the sun. Is there anything of which it may be said, see this is new? It has already been in ancient times before us.

Solomon is referring to the world of ideas when he talks about nothing being new under the sun. There is no philosophy of life that is completely new and there is no philosophy of life that is not related to Satan's approach to Eve in the fall in Genesis 3. We're going to take a look at that in a moment. And that is true of deconstruction. We have these new terms that are thrown at us and we think that somehow that's something completely new that the Bible doesn't address, but when we slow down, catch our breath and actually read the Bible we see that that's not the case. As a philosophy, deconstruction offers nothing new. It's something that the scriptures and the church have addressed through the centuries.

Ironically this philosophy of life that encourages people to put the Bible aside as an inspired and authoritative message from God confirms what the Bible says. Deconstruction confirms what the Bible says and we're going to take a look at that this morning.

So today we'll consider two things about deconstruction. The first is that it's not a new phenomenon — it has been around since the beginning of human existence, maybe called by different names. The church has addressed it countless times. It is not an existential threat to Christianity. The Lord Jesus Christ said 'I will build my church and the gates of hell will not prevail against it.' Having said all that, however, we also see that even though deconstruction as a philosophy is not new, it can have devastating effects on those who indulge in it and on those around them. That's what we call fallout.

## Review from Last Week

Let's start by reviewing last week. The definition of deconstruction: faith deconstruction is a postmodern process of rethinking your faith — and the last part is really the main part — without regarding Scripture as a standard. We all ought to examine our faith. That examination should always be done in light of the scriptures, not in a vacuum. The model of the Presbyterian and reformed churches is Ecclesia reformata, semper reformanda — the church reformed and always reforming. That means we are constantly looking at what we are doing to see if something needs to change. But we look toward the scriptures to see if something needs to change. Not to some other cultural phenomenon.

As Elder Anderson pointed out last week, deconstruction can be traced back to the 20th century French philosopher Jacques Derrida. His view of deconstruction meant dismantling our excessive loyalty to any idea and learning to see the aspects of the truth that might lie buried in its opposite. He didn't believe that words could be pinned down to a singular meaning — you have to figure out the meanings and then construct your meaning. Self becomes the standard for meaning.

When we are talking about deconstruction we are talking about the source of authority. Deconstruction has little to do with trying to achieve correct theology. There is no end or goal or destination to the deconstruction process. There is no external authority — self is the ultimate authority.

In the eyes of the deconstruction movement the greatest enemy of human well-being is evangelicalism. Blake Chastain has defined evangelicalism under five points and Elder Anderson covered those last week. These are all straw man caricature arguments. I dare say that most people who are deconstructing are not leaving the Christian faith as defined by the Bible. They're leaving some version of it, but not the one that's defined in the Bible.

## There Is Nothing New Under the Sun

Let's get this in our heads: there is nothing new under the sun, including deconstruction. While deconstruction is a new word applied to faith, the idea of deconstruction is as old as the garden. So deconstruction finds its origin in the fall.

Genesis chapter 3. The first thing we see is that when Satan approached Eve with the idea that God was holding out on her, he started by questioning what God actually said. Look at verse 1: Now the serpent was more cunning than any beast of the field which the Lord God had made. And he said to the woman, has God indeed said, you shall not eat of every tree of the garden? He starts questioning God's word. A deconstructed belief almost always begins with questions. Now questions are good — if they are honest. When it comes to faith, some questions seek answers. But other questions seek exit. Not an answer. When the question is being asked, the person already knows where they are going. That's a dishonest way to approach faith.

In our time, Satan continues to ask the same question in different contexts: Did God really say marriage is between one man and one woman for life? Did God really say that murder including abortion is wrong? Did God really say people go to hell for their sins? Did God really say that Jesus is the only way? Did God really say that there are only two genders that are biologically determined?

The second thing Satan does is that he moves from questioning God's word to denying it. Look at verse 4: Then the serpent said to the woman, you will not surely die. Satan says that what God said was untrue. Satan doesn't seek to prove God's nonexistence — he knows that's impossible. Rather, he undermines what God has said so that he can replace the truth with the lie. Romans 1:25 — these people exchanged the truth of God for the lie. The lie is that you are autonomous, that you can live apart from God. The truth that God is God for the lie that you are God.

Thirdly, Satan deconstructs who God is. Verses 4 through 6: God knows that in the day you eat of it, your eyes will be opened and you will be like God, knowing good and evil. Satan deconstructs the idea that God is loving by saying God is withholding something good from Eve. Satan deconstructs the idea that God is truthful by claiming that Eve won't die. Satan deconstructs the idea that God is sufficient by telling them that they need to be like God.

## Old and New Testament Examples

Deconstruction continues throughout the history of God's people. From Jeremiah 19: God was angry and ready to bring judgment on his people, because they let cultural beliefs, values, and practices of the pagan nations around them inform what they believed about God. They did not look to the scriptures to see what they believed about God. They looked at culture and decided to do what culture was doing. The Israelites surveyed the surrounding nations and concluded that God must like child sacrifice. Not only to false gods, but they were going to worship the true God by burning their children in the Temple. This ancient story relates to modern deconstruction in that we are all prone to imagine a God that is more like our culture or more like ourselves than who God truly is.

Paul warns Timothy about deconstruction in 1 Timothy — those who have wandered away, made shipwreck of their faith, denied the faith, abandoned their former faith, gone astray after Satan, swerved from the faith. Isn't this what deconstruction is?

A major reason that deconstruction is plaguing the church today is that the church is losing the knowledge of God. And because of that, the love for God. Christians today seem to be able to quote line after line from The Office, but they can't even find their way through the Bible. The only way to guard against false ideas about God is to fill our minds with true ideas about him.

What does somebody who says they are deconstructing do? First thing they will do is put the Bible away. And the second thing they will do is they are going to stop attending church. And yet they are in a quest for truth. I hope you see how those two things don't go together.

## The Fallout

The fact that deconstruction is not a new concept is helpful, but it does not undo the fallout that comes when someone deconstructs their faith. Those who deconstruct experience the death of their beliefs, their community, their confidence, their relationships, and more often than not their faith. Deconstruction follows the same tactics as the transgender movement. The stories that are put forward are stories where everybody's happy, everybody's doing great, it's the best thing they've done. But when you start talking to actual people, you can see that this is the most painful thing they've gone through in life.

Another 20th-century French philosopher — Jean-Paul Sartre — would start every semester, so the story goes, having students sign a paper before they continued the class, because if they followed his ideas to its logical conclusion, they might kill themselves before the semester was over. That is what deconstruction is doing — looking within on an existential trip. And at the end they're not going to be in a better place.

But deconstruction doesn't affect only the one going through it. It also impacts those who love the person who is going through it. The family as a whole is not better for it.

We must battle this idea and those who promote it. These people are not friends; they are instruments of Satan. The Apostle Paul tells elders in Titus chapter 1 verse 9, they are to hold fast to the faithful word as he has been taught that he may be able by sound doctrine to both exhort and convict those who contradict. The Bible is clear about those who draw others into sin and unbelief. These so-called deconstructing coaches are not your friend. This is what Jesus says of them: woe to him through whom offenses come. It will be better for him if a millstone were hung around his neck and he were thrown into the sea than that he should offend one of these little ones.

Paul calls these people wolves that if left unchecked will devour the flock: for I know this, that after my departure, savage wolves will come in among you, not sparing the flock. Also from among yourselves men will rise up, speaking perverse things to draw away the disciples after themselves. These spokespeople for the deconstruction movement are coming from the church. Exactly what Paul said.

You've heard the name John Piper. His son Abraham Piper abandoned the faith. And now he calls himself an evangelist for deconstruction. They are not happy with just doing it themselves. They want to convince you that you should do that as well.

We want to be honest about the fact that we are here to tell people where they should end up in their faith journey. We want to tell them: this is where you need to end up — not just to give them general rules for their journey — because it's not the journey that's important. It's the destination.

## Closing Prayer

Lord, thank you that you're a good God, a God who is true and the source of all authority. Help us to be content with you, because you're better than anything else the world has to offer. Strengthen our faith even as we worship you this morning. In Jesus' name. Amen.
